advantages: Exposes behavioural weaknesses (pacing, communication, recovery) that solo practice can't.
Builds the muscle memory of 'think aloud while coding' — the single biggest interview skill.
Forces realistic time pressure; coding on a clock is its own skill.
Provides candid feedback from a peer — vastly higher signal than self-assessment.
Surfaces the gap between 'I know this' and 'I can perform this'.
disadvantages: Time-intensive — a mock + debrief is 90 minutes; scheduling is a tax.
Quality of feedback depends heavily on the partner; a weak partner can give wrong advice.
Diminishing returns after ~10–15 mocks; over-practicing risks calcifying habits.
Mocks emphasize coding/whiteboarding skills; they don't fully replicate the stress of a real loop with stakes.
